findings.title: localStorage access during render throws in restricted browser contexts
findings.description: In `create-local-storage-hook.ts`, the `getStored()` function is called synchronously inside `useState(getStored)` during component render. It calls `localStorage.getItem(key)` without any try-catch guard. In Safari Private Browsing, Firefox with `privacy.firstparty.isolate`, or environments where the user has disabled storage access, `localStorage.getItem` throws a `SecurityError`. Because this is called during render (not in an effect), the exception propagates up uncaught and crashes the entire component tree. Every hook built on `createLocalStorageHook` (useAutoMarkRead, useDateMode, useLayout, useTheme, useMascot, etc.) — and by extension virtually every page in the app — is affected. The `useEffect` that writes back also calls `localStorage.setItem` unguarded, compounding the issue.
findings.recommendation: Wrap all localStorage access in try-catch and fall back to the default value. Example: `function getStored(): T { try { const stored = localStorage.getItem(key); if (stored !== null && allowed.has(stored)) return stored as T; } catch { /* storage unavailable */ } return defaultValue; }`. Apply the same pattern to the `setItem` call in the `useEffect`, and to `getAuthToken`/`setAuthToken` in `auth.ts`.

findings.title: markSeenOnServer can throw when both network and IndexedDB are unavailable
findings.description: In `markSeenWithQueue.ts`, `markSeenOnServer` catches the `apiPost` failure and falls back to `queueSeenIds`. However, `queueSeenIds` (which uses IndexedDB) is awaited without a try-catch. If IndexedDB is also unavailable — private browsing on Safari completely disables it, and some users block it — `queueSeenIds` will throw and the error propagates uncaught from `markSeenOnServer`. All call sites that do not themselves wrap this in a try-catch will surface an unhandled promise rejection. The function signature `Promise<void>` implies callers expect it to be safe to `await` without a catch, but this guarantee is broken.
findings.recommendation: Add an outer try-catch around the entire function body, or add a nested catch on the `queueSeenIds` call: `try { await queueSeenIds(ids) } catch { /* IndexedDB unavailable, ids lost */ }`. Since article-seen tracking is non-critical, failing silently is preferable to an unhandled rejection.

findings.title: App bootstrap crashes with no user feedback if root element is missing
findings.description: In `main.tsx`, `document.getElementById('root')!` uses a non-null assertion. If the HTML shell is misconfigured (CDN cache serving stale HTML, deployment race conditions, browser extensions removing the element), `getElementById` returns `null` and the `!` assertion makes TypeScript ignore it at compile time, but at runtime `createRoot(null)` throws `TypeError: Cannot read properties of null`. The error is uncaught and the user sees a blank page with no message. The `registerSW` call before it can also fail if the service worker API is unavailable.
findings.recommendation: Guard the mount point: `const rootEl = document.getElementById('root'); if (!rootEl) { document.body.textContent = 'Application failed to load. Please refresh.'; throw new Error('Root element #root not found'); }` Then pass `rootEl` to `createRoot`. This gives a meaningful error in logs and a visible message to the user.

findings.title: Invalid date strings produce 'Invalid Date' output rather than a safe fallback
findings.description: In `dateFormat.ts`, all three exported functions (`formatDate`, `formatRelativeDate`, `formatDetailDate`) guard against `null` and empty strings with `if (!iso) return ''`, but they do not guard against non-empty invalid date strings such as `'undefined'`, `'N/A'`, or malformed ISO values. `new Date('garbage')` produces an `Invalid Date` object; subsequent calls like `d.getFullYear()` return `null`, `d.getTime()` returns `null`, and `toLocaleDateString()` returns `'Invalid Date'` as a string in most browsers. Since these functions are called in article cards and list headers that are rendered for every article, a single bad `published_at` value from the server propagates as visible UI corruption across the feed.
findings.recommendation: After `const d = new Date(iso)`, add a validity check: `if (isNaN(d.getTime())) return ''`. This ensures any unparseable date string is treated the same as a missing date rather than producing a corrupted display string.

findings.title: Search HTTP errors silently return empty results, masking failures from callers
findings.description: In `search.ts`, non-503 non-OK HTTP responses fall through to `return { articles: [], has_more: false }` with no error signal. This means a 401, 500, or network timeout is indistinguishable from a legitimate search that returned zero results. The `SearchDialog` component (and other callers) will render an 'empty state' or 'no results' UI when the search index is actually broken or the user is unauthenticated. This is a misleading user experience and makes diagnosing outages harder. The 503 branch correctly returns `indexBuilding: true`, but all other errors are lost.
findings.recommendation: Throw an error (or return a typed `error` field) for non-OK, non-503 responses: `if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`Search failed: ${res.status}`)`. Callers should catch it and display an error state distinct from the zero-results state.

findings.title: useHighlightTheme and useArticleFont apply DOM side-effects with no cleanup on unmount
findings.description: Based on the symbol summary, `useHighlightTheme` calls `applyLink` (inserting `<link>` tags for CSS) and `useArticleFont` calls `getInitialValue`, both likely manipulating the DOM as side-effects. If these hooks are torn down and re-mounted (e.g., in React Strict Mode's double-invocation in development, or if the settings panel is unmounted), the `applyLink` function may leave orphaned `<link>` elements or CSS variables in the DOM that are never cleaned up. This can cause style bleed between renders or accumulated DOM nodes. The `use-highlight-theme.ts` has both `getInitialOverride` and `applyLink` as module-level functions invoked in effects — without confirmed cleanup returns, Strict Mode will expose this.
findings.recommendation: Ensure every `useEffect` that inserts `<link>` or `<style>` tags returns a cleanup function that removes the inserted element: `return () => linkEl.remove()`. Similarly, any CSS variable or class applied to `document.documentElement` should be reverted in the cleanup. Audit both hooks for missing effect cleanup returns.

findings.title: createLocalStorageHook does not synchronise across browser tabs
findings.description: The `createLocalStorageHook` factory returns a hook that reads from localStorage on mount and writes on change via `useEffect`, but it never subscribes to the `storage` window event. If the user has the app open in two tabs and changes a setting in one (e.g., dark mode, layout), the other tab's React state remains stale until it is refreshed. For simple preferences this is a minor annoyance, but for settings like `auto-mark-read` or `article-open-mode`, stale state in a background tab can cause unexpected behaviour when the user switches to it — e.g., articles not being marked read as expected. The `useDarkMode` hook partially handles this via `subscribeSystemDark` but that only covers the system media query, not the localStorage-stored preference.
findings.recommendation: Add a `storage` event listener in the hook's `useEffect`: `window.addEventListener('storage', handler)` where `handler` checks `e.key === key` and updates state if the new value is valid. Return `() => window.removeEventListener('storage', handler)` from the effect for cleanup.

findings.title: Service worker update toast uses null duration with no dismiss safeguard
findings.description: In `main.tsx`, the `onNeedRefresh` callback creates a toast with `duration: null`. If the `updateSW(true)` action callback throws or the service worker update fails, the toast remains permanently visible with no way to dismiss it (the only action is 'Reload'). Additionally, if `onNeedRefresh` fires multiple times (e.g., due to repeated SW registration), multiple permanent toasts stack up. The toast library (Sonner) does not deduplicate by default.
findings.recommendation: Assign a stable `id` to the toast (e.g., `id: 'sw-update'`) so Sonner deduplicates repeated calls. Wrap the `onClick` handler to catch errors from `updateSW(true)` and dismiss the toast on failure with an appropriate message: `onClick: async () => { try { await updateSW(true) } catch { toast.dismiss('sw-update'); toast.error('Update failed — please refresh manually.') } }`.

raw_output.summary:
The codebase is generally well-structured with good test coverage for individual hooks and utilities. However, several **robustness gaps** could cause silent failures or crashes in real-world conditions:

**Critical:** Both `create-local-storage-hook.ts` and `auth.ts` access `localStorage` synchronously during component render with no try-catch, crashing the entire app in Safari Private Browsing and other storage-restricted contexts. The `markSeenOnServer` fallback path can also throw unhandled rejections when IndexedDB is unavailable, breaking the seen-marking feature silently.

**Warning-level:** The `dateFormat` functions produce user-visible "Invalid Date" strings for malformed server data rather than falling back gracefully. The `search.ts` module swallows non-503 HTTP errors as empty results, making server-side failures invisible to users. The localStorage hook factory lacks cross-tab synchronisation, which can leave background tabs with stale settings state. DOM side-effects in `useHighlightTheme` and `useArticleFont` may lack cleanup on unmount.

**Info:** The service worker update toast has no deduplication ID and uses `duration: null` with no error handling in the action callback.

The most impactful fix is wrapping all `localStorage` access in try-catch (affects ~15+ hooks and auth across the entire app), followed by making `markSeenOnServer` fully safe to await without a caller-side catch.
